pattern: Bob only ever wins a section by shooting exactly one more arrow than Alice used there, so choosing which sections to contest reduces to a bounded knapsack over 12 items.
hints: For each scoring section, Bob either gives up on it completely, or spends the smallest number of arrows that guarantees a win there -- one more than however many Alice placed.
With only 12 sections, every possible subset of 'sections to contest' can be considered, since 2^12 is small; for each subset check whether the arrows it costs fit within the budget.

Track the subset of sections that gives the highest total score among all affordable subsets, then build Bob's arrow counts from it: the winning sections get Alice's count plus one, and any arrows left over can be dumped anywhere without hurting the score.

edge_inputs.description: minimum numArrows, Alice spent her only arrow on the top section
a modest spread across several sections
Alice dumped everything on the top section, leaving every lower section free to win cheaply
a wider, fairly even spread across the sections
Alice concentrated on the zero-point section, which is never worth contesting
exactly enough arrows to win half the sections at uniform cost, testing the knapsack tradeoff
one arrow is not enough to beat the only contested section, so it must be spent on an uncontested one instead

stress.description: a random split of numArrows across the 12 sections at the constraint maximum
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    num_arrows = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    alice = [0] * 12
    remaining = num_arrows
    for i in range(11):
        take = rng.randint(0, remaining)
        alice[i] = take
        remaining -= take
    alice[11] = remaining
    return [num_arrows, alice]
